questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Paragraph: Latimer diagrams show that standard reduction potentials connecting various oxidation states of an element. It shows the redox information about a series of species in a very condensed form. The latimer diagram about an element $X$ along with its oxidation states are given below
Question: Thermodynamically speaking, which of the given statement(s) is/are correct?
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& g(0.45)+1(1.07)=(g+1)(0.76) \\
& \Rightarrow g=1
\end{aligned}$

For $\mathrm{E}_2^{\circ}$
$\begin{aligned}
& 4 E_2^{\circ}+2(0.76)=6 \times 0.61 \\
& E_2^{\circ}=0.535 V
\end{aligned}$
and For $\mathrm{E}_1^{\circ}$
$\begin{aligned}
& 0.535 \times 4+0.45 \times 1=\mathrm{E}_1^{\circ} \times 5 \\
& \mathrm{E}_1^{\circ}=0.518 \mathrm{~V}
\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: For an electrode reaction written as $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{n}+}+\mathrm{ne}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{M} \\ & \mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}^{\circ}-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{nF}} \ln \frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{M}^{n+}\right]} \text { (Nernst equation) } \\ & =\mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{\mathrm{n}} \log \frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{M}^{n+}\right]} \text { at } 298 \mathrm{~K} \end{aligned}$ For the cell reaction, $a A+b B \rightleftharpoons x X+y Y$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^o-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{nF}} \ln \frac{[\mathrm{X}]^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{Y}]^{\mathrm{y}}}{[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{a}}[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{b}}} \\ & =\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{\mathrm{n}} \log \frac{[\mathrm{X}]^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{Y}]^{\mathrm{y}}}{[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{a}}[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{b}}} \text { at } 298 \mathrm{~K} \end{aligned}$ For pure solids, liquids or gases at 1 atm , molar concentration $=1$ Standard free energy change $\Delta G^{\circ}=-n E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ} F$ where $n$ is the number of electrons transferred in the redox reaction of the cell, $\mathrm{E}_{\text {oell }}^{\circ}$ is the standard emf of the cell. F stands for 1 Faraday, i.e. $96500 \mathrm{C} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ (approx.). Standard free energy change $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=\frac{-2.303 R T}{\mathrm{nF}} \log _{\mathrm{eq}}$. Where $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}$ is the equilibrium constant at TK. $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}$ can be calculated from $E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}$ by using the relation, $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\frac{0.0591}{n} \log K_{\text {eq }}$Question: On the basis of information available from the reaction $\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{Al}+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \frac{2}{3} \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3, \Delta \mathrm{G}=-827 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ The minimum emf required to carry out an electrolysis of $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ is $\left(\mathrm{F}=96500 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution:
For 1 mole of $\mathrm{O}_2$
$\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{O}^{2-}\left(\text { i.e. } \frac{2}{3} \times 3 \mathrm{O}^{2-}\right)$

Or $\frac{4}{3}$ mol of Al to change into $\mathrm{Al}^{+3}$ ions.
$n=4$

Thus, $\Delta \mathrm{G}=-\mathrm{nEF}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{E}=-\frac{\Delta \mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{nF}}=\frac{+827000}{4 \times 96500}=2.14 \mathrm{volt}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.q: Paragraph: For an electrode reaction written as $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{n}+}+\mathrm{ne}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{M} \\ & \mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}^{\circ}-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{nF}} \ln \frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{M}^{n+}\right]} \text { (Nernst equation) } \\ & =\mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{\mathrm{n}} \log \frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{M}^{n+}\right]} \text { at } 298 \mathrm{~K} \end{aligned}$ For the cell reaction, $a A+b B \rightleftharpoons x X+y Y$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^o-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{nF}} \ln \frac{[\mathrm{X}]^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{Y}]^{\mathrm{y}}}{[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{a}}[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{b}}} \\ & =\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{\mathrm{n}} \log \frac{[\mathrm{X}]^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{Y}]^{\mathrm{y}}}{[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{a}}[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{b}}} \text { at } 298 \mathrm{~K} \end{aligned}$ For pure solids, liquids or gases at 1 atm , molar concentration $=1$ Standard free energy change $\Delta G^{\circ}=-n E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ} F$ where $n$ is the number of electrons transferred in the redox reaction of the cell, $\mathrm{E}_{\text {oell }}^{\circ}$ is the standard emf of the cell. F stands for 1 Faraday, i.e. $96500 \mathrm{C} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ (approx.). Standard free energy change $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=\frac{-2.303 R T}{\mathrm{nF}} \log _{\mathrm{eq}}$. Where $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}$ is the equilibrium constant at TK. $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}$ can be calculated from $E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}$ by using the relation, $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\frac{0.0591}{n} \log K_{\text {eq }}$Question: The e.m.f. of the cell $\mathrm{Zn}\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{+2}(0.01 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Fe}^{+2}(0.001 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{Fe}$ at 298 K is 0.2905 V . The value of the equilibrium constant for cell reaction is:
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

The equilibrium constant for the cell reaction $\mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{Cu}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{Zn}_{\text {(eq) }}^{+2}$ is of the order of

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: If a cell has cell potential ' $E$ ' and standard cell potential ' $E^0$ ', then free energy change of cell process may be calculated as, $\Delta G=-W=-n F E \text { and } \Delta G^{\circ}=-W_{\max }=-n F E^{\circ}$ Where ' $n$ ' is the number of electrons involved in overall all process. According to Gibs-Helmholtz equation: $\begin{aligned} \Delta \mathrm{G} & =\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S} \\ \Delta \mathrm{G} & =\Delta \mathrm{H}+\mathrm{T}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} \Delta \mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{dT}}\right)_P \end{aligned}$ Temperature coefficient of cell ' $\mu$ ' will be equal to $\left(\frac{d E}{d T}\right)_P$Question: $\Delta G^0$ for the Daniell cell $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{s})\left|\mathrm{ZnSO}_4\right||\mathrm{CuSO}| \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s}) \\ & \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}}^{\circ}=-0.76 \mathrm{~V} ; \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}=+0.34 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$ Will be:
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: If a cell has cell potential ' $E$ ' and standard cell potential ' $E^0$ ', then free energy change of cell process may be calculated as, $\Delta G=-W=-n F E \text { and } \Delta G^{\circ}=-W_{\max }=-n F E^{\circ}$ Where ' $n$ ' is the number of electrons involved in overall all process. According to Gibs-Helmholtz equation: $\begin{aligned} \Delta \mathrm{G} & =\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S} \\ \Delta \mathrm{G} & =\Delta \mathrm{H}+\mathrm{T}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} \Delta \mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{dT}}\right)_P \end{aligned}$ Temperature coefficient of cell ' $\mu$ ' will be equal to $\left(\frac{d E}{d T}\right)_P$Question: The temperature coefficient of a cell whose cell reaction is $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Pb}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{HgCl}_2(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{PbCl}_2(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Hg}(\ell) \\ & \left(\frac{\mathrm{dE}}{\mathrm{dT}}\right)_{\mathrm{P}}=1.5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{vK}^{-1} \text { at } 298 \mathrm{~K} \end{aligned}$ The change in entropy in $\mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ for the given cell reaction will be
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
